title: React.js Core Concepts
description: Interview-ready flashcards covering React fundamentals: declarative UI, components, virtual DOM, JSX, hooks, state management, lifecycle, and best practices.
checklist: Understand React's declarative approach and component architecture
Explain Virtual DOM and reconciliation process
Describe state, props, and their differences
Understand lifecycle methods and React Hooks
Explain context API and state management patterns
Understand JSX, Babel, and modern React workflows
answerHtml:
Sub QuestionAnswerMore ExplanationExampleBenefit
What is React?A JavaScript library for building user interfaces with componentsReact focuses on creating reusable, self-contained pieces of UIBuild dashboard with Navbar, Sidebar, MainContent componentsReusable, maintainable code
What is declarative UI?You describe the desired UI state; React handles DOM updatesEasier to understand and debug than imperative codeWrite JSX describing the UI, React updates DOM automaticallyCleaner, more readable code
What is component-based architecture?React apps are built using reusable, self-contained componentsEach component manages its own state and logicUserCard, ProductList, SearchBar are individual componentsModularity and code reuse
What is the Virtual DOM?An in-memory representation of the actual DOMReact calculates minimal updates needed and applies them efficientlyReact compares virtual DOM tree, identifies differences, updates real DOMOptimized rendering performance
How does Virtual DOM reconciliation work?React compares old and new virtual DOM trees (diffing) and updates only necessary partsMinimizes direct manipulations to real DOM for efficiencyList items change: React updates only modified items, not the entire listBetter performance and user experience
What is JSX?JavaScript XML - a syntax extension allowing HTML-like syntax in JavaScriptJSX simplifies creation of React elements and improves readability<div className="card"><h1>{title}</h1></div>More readable and maintainable code
How is JSX transformed?Babel transpiles JSX into JavaScript function calls (React.createElement)Browser doesn't understand JSX, so Babel converts it to compatible code<Button onClick={handleClick}/> becomes React.createElement(Button, {onClick: handleClick})Write cleaner code, run anywhere
What is unidirectional data flow?Data flows in one direction: parent to child through propsMaintains predictable state and makes changes easier to trackParent: <Child data={value}/>; Child receives and uses dataPredictable, easier to debug
What is the difference between state and props?State: internal, mutable data; Props: external, immutable data from parentState causes re-render when changed; props are read-only in childState: this.state = {count: 0}; Props: <Counter initialValue={5}/>Clear data management boundaries
Can you modify props in a child component?No, props are immutable and read-only in child componentsTo update, call a callback function passed by parentParent: <Child onUpdate={setName}/>; Child: onClick={() => onUpdate(newValue)}Prevents unintended data mutations
What are lifecycle methods?Methods called at different stages of component existence: mounting, updating, unmountingAllow you to run code at specific times in component lifecyclecomponentDidMount: fetch data; componentWillUnmount: cleanup subscriptionsControl component behavior at key moments
What is componentDidMount()?Lifecycle method called after component is rendered on screenGood place for network requests, timers, and subscriptionsFetch user data from API when component loadsExecute setup logic after component ready
What is componentDidUpdate()?Lifecycle method invoked after component updatesReceives previous props and state; useful for side effectsUpdate page title when props change, log state updatesRespond to prop/state changes after rendering
What is componentWillUnmount()?Lifecycle method called before component is removed from DOMEssential for cleanup: cancel requests, unsubscribe, clear timersUnsubscribe from socket, cancel API requests, clear intervalsPrevent memory leaks
What are React Hooks?Functions allowing functional components to have state and lifecycle methodsIntroduced in React 16.8; alternative to class componentsuseState for state, useEffect for side effects, useContext for contextSimpler, cleaner code; easier to reuse logic
What is useState()?Hook for adding state to functional componentsReturns [value, setter] pair; setter updates state and triggers re-renderconst [count, setCount] = useState(0); then setCount(count + 1)Simple state management in functional components
What is useEffect()?Hook for performing side effects in functional componentsRuns after render; replaces componentDidMount, componentDidUpdate, componentWillUnmountuseEffect(() => { fetchData(); }, []); fetch runs once on mountClean up effects; avoid memory leaks
What is the dependency array in useEffect?Array of dependencies; effect runs when dependencies changeEmpty array: runs once on mount; no array: runs after every render; with deps: runs when deps changeuseEffect(() => { fetch(userId); }, [userId]); runs when userId changesControl when effects run; optimize performance
What is useContext()?Hook to consume context values without prop drillingAllows components to access shared state directly from Contextconst theme = useContext(ThemeContext); use theme.color directlyAvoid prop drilling through multiple levels
What is useMemo()?Hook to memoize computation results; prevents unnecessary recalculationsReturns memoized value; recalculates only when dependencies changeconst expensiveValue = useMemo(() => complexCalculation(data), [data])Optimize performance for expensive operations
What is useCallback()?Hook to memoize function; prevents unnecessary function re-creationReturns memoized callback; useful for passing to optimized child componentsconst handleClick = useCallback(() => { doSomething(); }, [dependency])Prevent child re-renders caused by function prop changes
What is useRef()?Hook creating mutable reference that persists across rendersCommon use: access DOM elements directly, store mutable valuesconst inputRef = useRef(null); then inputRef.current.focus()Direct DOM access; persist values without causing re-renders
What is the Context API?Built-in React feature allowing components to share state without prop drillingCreate context, wrap tree with provider, consume with useContext or ConsumerCreate ThemeContext, pass theme through Provider to all descendantsManage global state simply without external libraries
What is prop drilling?Passing props through multiple intermediate components to reach target componentBecomes unwieldy with deeply nested componentsApp → Page → Section → Card → Button receives prop from AppContext API and state management libraries solve this
How do you handle forms in React?Use state to manage input values; handle onChange and onSubmit eventsControlled components use state as source of truthconst [email, setEmail] = useState(''); <input value={email} onChange={(e) => setEmail(e.target.value)} />Controlled inputs; easy validation and submission
What is a controlled component?Component whose value is controlled by React stateForm input value comes from state; onChange updates stateInput value displays state; typing updates state; state updates inputFull control over input behavior
What is an uncontrolled component?Component managing its own state internally; accessed via refDOM is source of truth; less common in Reactconst nameRef = useRef(); then access nameRef.current.value in handlerSimpler for simple inputs; less React integration
How does React handle events?Use camelCase event handlers (onClick, onChange, onSubmit)React uses event delegation; handlers are synthetic events<button onClick={handleClick}>Click</button>Consistent cross-browser event handling
What are keys and why are they important in lists?Unique identifiers for list items helping React recognize which items changedKeys improve performance and maintain component state in dynamic lists<li key={item.id}>{item.name}</li> instead of key={index}Correct rendering and state management in lists
Why shouldn't you use array index as key?If list reorders, index no longer matches the same item, breaking component stateComponent state gets tied to wrong item; causes bugs and performance issuesReorder list: index 0 now refers to different item; state gets mixed upUse unique IDs as keys
What is React.memo?Higher-order component preventing re-render if props haven't changedOptimizes performance by skipping render for unchanged propsexport default React.memo(MyComponent); skips render if props sameReduce unnecessary re-renders
What is a higher-order component (HOC)?Function taking component and returning enhanced componentPattern for code reuse, logic abstraction, cross-cutting concernswithTheme(Component) wraps component providing theme propsReuse logic across multiple components
What is render props pattern?Pattern where component accepts function as prop to render contentAlternative to HOC for sharing component logic<DataProvider render={data => <Component data={data} />} />Flexible component composition
How do you communicate React frontend with .NET Core backend?Use HTTP requests (fetch or Axios) to call REST APIs on backendReact components make requests; backend returns JSON datafetch('/api/users').then(res => res.json()).then(data => setUsers(data))Seamless frontend-backend integration
What is Axios?Popular HTTP client library for making API requestsCleaner syntax than fetch; better error handling; automatic transformationsaxios.get('/api/data').then(res => setData(res.data))Simpler HTTP handling
How do you handle errors in API calls?Use try-catch with async/await or .catch() with promisesGracefully handle network errors, timeouts, server errorscatch(error => setError(error.message)); display error to userBetter user experience
What is Redux?Predictable state container for JavaScript apps; manages centralized app stateStores entire state in single store; dispatch actions to trigger state changesStore contains user, posts, ui state; dispatch({type: 'SET_USER', payload})Predictable state management for complex apps
What are Redux actions?Plain objects describing state changes; must have type propertyOther properties carry data (payload) needed for state update{type: 'ADD_TODO', payload: {id: 1, text: 'Learn Redux'}}Clear intent of state changes
What are Redux reducers?Pure functions taking current state and action, returning new stateMust be pure; no side effects; always return new state(state = [], action) => action.type === 'ADD_TODO' ? [...state, action.payload] : statePredictable state transitions
What is useSelector in Redux?Hook to select state from Redux store in functional componentsEfficiently subscribes to store; re-renders only when selected state changesconst todos = useSelector(state => state.todos)Access Redux state cleanly in components
What is useDispatch in Redux?Hook to dispatch actions from functional componentsReturns dispatch function to trigger state changesconst dispatch = useDispatch(); dispatch(addTodo(newTodo))Trigger state changes from components
What is React Router?Library for client-side routing in React SPAsEnables navigation between pages without full page reloads<BrowserRouter> <Route path="/home" component={Home} /> </BrowserRouter>Seamless single-page app navigation
What is the difference between SSR and CSR?SSR: renders components on server, sends HTML to client; CSR: renders on client with JavaScriptSSR better for SEO and initial load; CSR better for user interactionsSSR: Next.js renders page on server; CSR: React renders page in browserChoose based on app needs
What is code splitting?Breaking app into smaller chunks loaded on demand instead of one big bundleImproves initial load performance; chunks loaded when neededReact.lazy() + Suspense for component splitting; Webpack dynamic importsFaster initial page load
What is lazy loading?Deferring loading of components until they're neededImproves performance; reduces initial bundle sizeconst Home = React.lazy(() => import('./Home')); wrapped in SuspenseBetter performance
What is Webpack?Module bundler combining JavaScript, CSS, images into optimized bundlesHandles code splitting, minification, asset managementWebpack bundles all modules into dist/bundle.jsOptimized production builds
What is Babel?JavaScript compiler transpiling modern ES6+ and JSX into browser-compatible codeEnables use of latest features while supporting older browsersBabel transforms <Component /> JSX into React.createElement(Component)Write modern code; run everywhere
What is TypeScript in React?Adds static typing to JavaScript; catches errors during developmentBetter tooling support, autocomplete, early error detectioninterface User { name: string; age: number; }; const user: User = {}Fewer bugs; better developer experience
What are custom hooks?Functions creating reusable stateful logic extracted from componentsCustom hooks are JavaScript functions starting with 'use'function useFetch(url) { const [data, setData] = useState(null); useEffect(() => {...}); return data; }Reuse complex logic across components
What is the useReducer hook?Hook for complex state logic; manages state with reducer functionBetter than useState for related state pieces; similar to Redux reducersconst [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, initialState); dispatch({type: 'ACTION'})Manage complex state cleanly
What is CORS?Cross-Origin Resource Sharing - policy controlling cross-domain requests.NET Core backend must allow requests from React frontend domain.NET configure CORS: app.UseCors(builder => builder.AllowAnyOrigin())Secure yet flexible API access
How do you secure React-to-.NET communication?Use HTTPS, JWT tokens for authentication, validate permissions on backendStore JWT in localStorage/cookies; send in Authorization headerPOST /auth/login returns JWT; subsequent requests include token headerProtect sensitive data and user actions
What is authentication in React?Process of verifying user identity using credentials (username/password, OAuth, JWT)Backend validates credentials; returns token React stores and usesLogin: send credentials to backend; backend returns JWT tokenSecure user access
What is authorization in React?Process of determining what authenticated user can access/doBackend checks user roles/permissions; React conditionally renders based on rolesAdmin sees delete button; user doesn't; check backend permissionsControl user permissions
What is responsive design in React?Design approach making apps work on all device sizesUse CSS media queries, flexible layouts, and viewport meta tagCSS: @media (max-width: 768px) { .sidebar { display: none; } }Works on desktop, tablet, mobile
What is Bootstrap?CSS framework providing pre-styled components and responsive gridIncludes components like navbars, modals, buttons; responsive utilities<div className="container"> <div className="row"> <div className="col-md-6">Faster UI development
What is Material UI?React UI framework following Google Material Design principlesProvides React components with Material Design look and feelimport { Button, Card } from '@mui/material'; <Button variant="contained">Professional, consistent UI
What is performance optimization in React?Techniques reducing rendering, improving load time, and user experienceCode splitting, memoization, lazy loading, efficient state managementReact.memo prevents unnecessary renders; useMemo caches computationsFaster app, better UX
